name: Ceres
description: The only dwarf planet in the inner Solar System, located in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter. It was originally classified as a planet when discovered in 1801, then reclassified as an asteroid, and finally as a dwarf planet in 2006.

name: Eris
discoverer: Mike Brown, Chad Trujillo, David Rabinowitz
description: The most massive known dwarf planet and the most distant object visited by a spacecraft-tracked trajectory. Its discovery directly led to the reclassification of Pluto in 2006. Eris lies in the scattered disc, far beyond the Kuiper Belt.

name: Gonggong
discoverer: Megan Schwamb, Mike Brown, David Rabinowitz
description: Formally designated 2007 OR10 and later named Gonggong, this large scattered disc object is considered a likely dwarf planet due to its size. It has a highly eccentric orbit and a reddish surface, possibly from tholins.

name: Haumea
discoverer: Mike Brown et al. (Caltech) / José Luis Ortiz et al. (IAA)
description: A distinctively elongated dwarf planet with a rapid rotation of about 3.9 hours, giving it an ellipsoidal shape. Haumea has a ring system and two known moons, and its surface is composed largely of crystalline water ice.

name: Makemake
discoverer: Mike Brown, Chad Trujillo, David Rabinowitz
description: The second largest Kuiper Belt Object after Pluto. Makemake has a very bright reddish surface coated with methane, ethane, and nitrogen ices. It has one known small moon discovered in 2016.

name: Orcus
discoverer: Mike Brown, Chad Trujillo, David Rabinowitz
description: A large trans-Neptunian object with an orbit very similar to Pluto's, leading some to call it the anti-Pluto. Orcus has a large moon, Vanth, with a mass ratio suggesting a possible collisional origin similar to the Pluto-Charon system.

name: Pluto
description: The largest known Kuiper Belt Object and the most famous dwarf planet, reclassified from planet to dwarf planet by the IAU in 2006. Pluto has a complex geology with mountains, plains, and a thin nitrogen atmosphere. The New Horizons spacecraft performed a close flyby in July 2015.

name: Quaoar
description: A classical Kuiper Belt Object large enough to be considered a likely dwarf planet. In 2023, a dense ring system was discovered around Quaoar, orbiting at a distance where rings were not thought to be stable—a surprising finding for planetary science.

name: Sedna
discoverer: Mike Brown, Chad Trujillo, David Rabinowitz
description: The most distant known object in the Solar System at its current position, orbiting in what may be the inner Oort Cloud. Sedna has an extremely elongated orbit with a period of about 11,400 years. Its origin—whether captured from another star system or native—remains debated.

name: Salacia
discoverer: Henry Roe, Michael Brown, Kristina Barkume
description: A large classical Kuiper Belt Object considered a likely dwarf planet candidate. Salacia has an unusually low albedo for an icy body, suggesting a surface mixed with dark material. Its moon Actaea has allowed a precise mass determination.
